speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Iris Lasky, I-R-I-S L-A-S-K-Y. I'm interviewing Regina Goldstein. We are in the city of North Hills, California, country of the United States of America. Today's date is July 10th, 1995. And our-- our interview is being done in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll say rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I will say speed and I will say--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait. Hold up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling. Stand by.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina, would you please start by giving us your full name today and spelling it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina Goldstein, R-E-G-I-N-A G-O-L-D-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name given to you at your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Golda Finkelstein, G-O-L-D-A F-I-N-K-E-L-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Kurow, Poland, November 12th, 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Kurow?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-U-R-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been known by any other names other than what you've just shared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, while living on a Ukrainian farm as a Gentile, I was known by the name of Eugenia, E-U-G-E-N-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other last names that you've been known?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- none that I recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little about your town, Kurow, what you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small shtetl like many others in Poland and had a very vibrant Jewish community, businesses, schools. Our synagogue there had been built in 1605, so we had been in the region for all those years. And they had a kindergarten that I went to. I don't remember too much more about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my-- my mother, my father, and I. I was an only child. They each had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] six brothers and sisters on each side. There were seven children in each family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Four sisters, three brothers on my mother's side and four brothers and three sisters on my father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Schmuel Finkelstein. He was one of the most educated men in the city. Very few Jews from small shtetls went to universities. My father was sent to a university in Wilno, studied rabbinical things. Changed his mind and went on to law and studied law, came back to town. And my grandfather on my father's side was in the leather business. They made shoelaces which were shipped all over the country. And he was involved in that as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Hena. Her maiden name was Rosenson. She also came from a family that was in the business in leather. They produced shoes, but only the top of the shoes. The soles were sent somewhere else to be put on. My grandfather, David Rosenson, was the head of the family on my mother's side. And my three uncles, Chaim, Aryeih, and Sana, worked with him. My uncle Sana was the youngest of seven children. My uncle Aryeih, who was the middle son, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] left Poland at the very last minute in 1939 and went to Israel and lived there through the war. And he fought with Menachem Begin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your religious upbringing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's parents were Orthodox, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very religious. My mother's-- well, my grandmother had already passed away before I was born. But my grandfather David was a little bit more lenient. His girls didn't have to cut their hair when they got married. And he-- more probably to the Conservative side, as we know it now, rather than the Orthodox. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We, of course, celebrated and observed all the holidays. My grandparents, my parents went to shul on Saturday mornings. We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put the food into the ovens on Friday afternoon and took it out after-- after synagogue on-- on Saturday afternoons. It was-- it was a nice life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the closeness of the two families?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they intermingled. They-- this I-- this is what I hear now, because being a child, I don't remember all this. But I remember going to different houses at different times and having people come to our house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm told that my-- my parents' house was full of intellectuals. And they used to come and discuss world affairs, which I, of course, didn't partake in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any special fond memories as a child with your families?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go on picnics sometimes. I remember an old tree that had little pears. That seems to be sticking out in my memory a lot. Certain items from the house. But we-- I left the house, never to see it again, when I was four years old, so it's just little items.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about school friends or young friends at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I went to a preschool. There were many, you know, children there. I'm sorry to say that none of them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I know of, are alive. I don't remember their names anymore. And that's-- that's about it about the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall hearing that your parents or family belonged to any organizations, whether Zionist or other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother's family was very involved in Zionism. My uncles were followers of Jabotinsky. They belonged to Betar. And they were-- my uncles especially were urging us to-- to emigrate to Palestine, not just us, but the families. But it's hard to pick up and leave when you have roots and families. And-- and you don't listen to the younger people. I've learned that in my life. So we stayed, except for my uncle Aryeih, who left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall hearing any news of what was going on in Germany and the rest of the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall what was being said. But I remember people sitting around a radio in our house and listening and discussing what could happen, what might happen. But I don't think anyone ever envisioned anything as to what actually did happen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father, I was told by a friend of his that I saw in Israel in 1982, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] said that my father felt that even if the Germans occupied Poland, they were very civilized people. And he-- he didn't think it would be so terrible. He-- this friend had gone deeper into Russia, and that's how he survived the war. And he urged him to go with him. But my father was so anti-Communist that he didn't want to have anything to do with Stalin. And he felt that-- that the chances for us, ironically, were better under the civilized Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever recall hearing or experiencing any anti-Semitism in Kurow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't recall that as a child. But I did experience it in-- right after the war, in 1944 and '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever heard about the anti-Semitism within Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, yes. Not as a child. Not as-- you know, prior to 1939. If there was any, I, being a child, was not aware of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if Kurow was ever subject to pogroms or any type of disturbances by the Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing of-- of terrible consequences. There was always, you know, the name-calling and the things like that. But I was never told of anything that was really extremely serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you recall, in your own recollection, the changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember the bombing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My aunt Frances and her husband and child had-- were living in France at the time. And they had come to visit. And we were going on one of our picnics when the planes came out of the sky. And I remember it turning black. And the bombs started to fall. And within hours, our town was burning. We were in the fields, high, you know, by the road in ditches. And after that, we gathered in I don't know whose house it was. Maybe it was my grandfather's. But most of the town was in ruins and burning. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] life changed after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was September 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did life then change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it changed because we were confined to a certain area almost immediately. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The freedom that I had had before didn't exist. I didn't go back to school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were-- the grown-ups seemed to be changed in a way that filtered through to the children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They started-- my uncles, especially my uncle Chaim, wanted to leave German-occupied Poland to go across to the part that the Russians occupied. And it was decided that part of the family would go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The older people didn't want to go. One of my aunts had a newborn child. She couldn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the family split. And my grandfather David and the two-- Chaim and Sana, the two sons and the three daughters and their husbands and the children went. We went across the border at night by bribery. I-- I don't know how.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we wandered around in Russian-occupied Poland for a while. And we settled in a town, Zofjowka. And that's where we lived. My grandfather and-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had taken some of the machines that they used in making the shoes with them so that they were able to set up a business of sorts to survive. And we lived there until the Germans marched in in 1941 and we found ourselves right back where we started. At that point, we were almost immediately put into a ghetto in the town. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we-- we lived like in a two-room place. I remember we had, I believe, one Passover there. And I remember the women getting together and baking the matzas in one place, where they made it clean for Passover, and then distributing it to the houses. And it was hanging from a-- in a sheet from the-- from the ceiling. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my uncle Chaim had always been-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, I don't know, maybe a little wiser than a lot of us. And because the Germans made my grandfather make shoes for them, he was allowed to leave the ghetto to deliver them to their post or-- and he was-- when he was able to leave the ghetto, he made it a-- a point to learn as much as he could. And when there were rumblings of something happening, he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he got part of our family out into the forests. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he-- he and several others started a partisan group in the area. We-- we got out into the forest in the-- in the summer of 1942. But that September, my grandfather, who was religious and wanted to go back to the ghetto for the high holidays, went back. My uncle Chaim was in the forest with the partisans. But my younger-- the younger son, Sana, went with him. And that-- they liquidated the ghetto on Yom Kippur. My uncle Sana was made, with other younger men, to dig the ditches. And my grandfather was shot while running away. My uncle was put on a truck to go to the trains. And he-- he and another and a cousin of his got away in the melee. And he joined my other uncle as a partisan in the forests. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Right around that time, my uncle Chaim came back to where we were living in the forest. And what-- what we did at that time is to make like a room underground so that during the daytime, if anybody wandered through the forest, you-- it looked like there was no one there. And we would come out at night. And he came one day and he said that he had found a couple who would keep me, that we-- he had paid them with our household goods, that-- whatever we had-- he had taken out of the ghetto little by little on all-- on all the times that were when he was getting in and out, and I could go and live with them in the open, where I wouldn't have to live underground. He also said that he had found a place where my mother and father and my aunt would be able to be hidden by another Ukrainian. And at that point, I was taken to this couple. The lady's name is Anna. I don't remember her husband's name. They were a couple in their 40s. And he left me there and told me that he would come and visit me from time to time. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was the-- he did. I gather from-- from something that happened later that my parents and my aunt were hidden by someone for a little while. But then he was-- he-- he told them to go. My parents came to visit me three times through this period. And the last time was in the early winter of-- of '43, maybe-- maybe December '42, maybe January '43. And they stayed for one night. And then they left. And that was the last time I ever saw them. A few weeks later, one of the partisans came and told the people that I was living with that my parents and my aunt had been caught by a German patrol and shot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My uncle Chaim died later that year. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at this point, I felt that all my contacts with my family had broken off.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were how old at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 6, 7. You learn early not to talk out of turn, not to-- you know, pretend you don't know things that are going on around you. At one point on the farm, the Germans were doing an almost house-to-house search. And I-- the-- the lady took me to a group of partisans. And I was with them for approximately two weeks, constantly on the move. Things were very bad that winter. And when I came back a couple of weeks later, my hair had not been combed for the two weeks. She had to cut it all off. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And things got a little easier on the farm at that point. They had stopped the searches. And I thought that I would probably be there forever. I didn't know. You know, children don't really think too far ahead, I guess. But on one of her trips into the forest, Anna fell into one of those underground houses. And there was a Jewish family hiding there. And they were from the ghetto that I had been in. And she told them that they were welcome to come every now and then into the house when they were sure that there was no one around. You must understand that we're talking about a little house surrounded by forests. And it was hard even to see the next farm. It was like a-- a half a day's or more ride away, so that in the dark, it was possible to come. And they did and-- several times, to sit in a house for a little while and have hot tea and some food. She-- they saw me and I saw them. But there was no acknowledgement. But it must have been fate that they did, because they ran into my uncle Sana, who survived as a partisan and was in Kiev at the time. And they had ended up there. They asked him if he had me with him, and he said he didn't know where I was. So they brought him to the farm. I'm a little bit ahead of myself. I-- the Russians liberated that part of Ukrainia [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the-- in the fall of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, the spring of '44 or the-- somewhere in there. And so that when he came, it was very shortly after that, maybe a couple of months. And at first, I didn't want to go with him, because when my other uncle died, they had said that six partisans died, and two of them were Jewish. And the last that I had known, my uncle Sana and my uncle Chaim were together. So it was hard for me to accept that he was really there. But he stayed a couple of days on the farm with me. And then the lady went with us back to Kiev, Anna. And she left me there with him. And we lived in Kiev with a couple, Grisha and-- and his wife, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] who were also partisans. My uncle had been wounded and heroically decorated. But when the part of Poland where we come from, which is near Lublin, was liberated by the Russians, my uncle asked permission to go to Lublin to see if we had any survivors there. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] It's-- it's hard to remember everything and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little bit. Let's go back to you going into the forest and remembering your grandfather and uncle wanting to go back. Do you remember the scene?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- yes, I do. My-- my grandfather announced that he was going back for the high holiday services. And everyone started arguing with him, that we had gotten out of the ghetto and why was he doing this? And he said that it was important for him to be there. He-- he had never in his whole life missed a Kol Nidrei service, and he didn't intend to do it this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we could not convince him not to go, you know, my parents and the rest of the family. And he went. And of course, when everything was all right on Rosh Hashanah, we were hoping that everything would be OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they decided to liquidate the ghetto on Yom Kippur. And they came early in the morning and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] surrounded the ghetto. And this is from what I understand. I obviously wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had been living in the ghetto prior to, of course, leaving for the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the life of the ghetto at that time that you can remember or have been told.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't easy. The-- the people lived in fear. There were rumors going around of terrible things happening. And people-- so many people had no work. There was nothing for them to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember my father sitting and reading a lot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You-- you can't-- you can't go for a walk this time of day. You can't do this that time of day. There was communal eating, sharing of food. The-- May I? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut.
